variables.r: Distance from current element to field point (m)
variables.r_hat: Unit vector from current element to field point
variables.n: Number of turns per unit length (for solenoid context)

locked_facts: Magnetic field due to infinite straight wire: B = μ₀I/(2πr)
Magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop: B = μ₀I/(2R)
Magnetic field on the axis of a circular loop: B = μ₀IR²/[2(R² + x²)^(3/2)]
Direction of dB is given by the cross product dl × r̂ (right-hand screw rule)
The Biot-Savart law is the magnetic analogue of Coulomb's law — both are inverse-square laws
truth_statements: The magnetic field dB due to a current element Idl is perpendicular to both dl and the position vector r, as given by the cross product
The magnitude of dB is proportional to I, dl, sin θ and inversely proportional to r², where θ is the angle between dl and r
For an infinite straight conductor, integrating Biot-Savart gives B = μ₀I/(2πr), with field lines as concentric circles
At the centre of a circular loop of radius R carrying current I, the field is B = μ₀I/(2R), directed along the axis of the loop
The Biot-Savart law holds for all steady (non-time-varying) currents and is experimentally verified

aha_moment.what_to_show: A circular loop with current I — each small segment dl contributes a dB vector at the centre. All dB vectors from symmetric segments point in the SAME direction (along the axis), so they all add up constructively. Compare with a straight wire where dB contributions from different segments partially cancel.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: All dB arrows at the loop centre point upward along the axis — no cancellation, maximum constructive addition. The formula B = μ₀I/(2R) appears as the result of this perfect alignment.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think the field at the centre should point radially or along the wire — they see that cross-product geometry forces every dB to align along the axis

simulation_states.label: A current element in space
simulation_states.description: A single current element Idl shown as a glowing segment with current direction arrow. The position vector r from dl to a field point P is drawn. No field shown yet.
simulation_states.emphasis: Establish the geometry — current element dl, position vector r, and the angle θ between them

simulation_states.label: dB from cross product
simulation_states.description: The dB vector appears at point P, perpendicular to both dl and r (out of the plane formed by dl and r). The cross-product geometry is highlighted — dl × r̂ gives the direction. Formula overlay: dB = (μ₀/4π)(Idl sin θ)/r².
simulation_states.emphasis: dB is perpendicular to the plane of dl and r — direction from right-hand screw rule. Magnitude depends on sin θ: maximum when θ = 90°, zero when θ = 0°

simulation_states.label: Full circular loop — all dB vectors align
simulation_states.description: A complete circular loop with current I. Multiple dl segments are highlighted around the loop. Each contributes a dB at the centre — ALL dB vectors point along the axis (upward). They add constructively to give B = μ₀I/(2R). A straight wire is shown for comparison where dB vectors partially cancel.
simulation_states.emphasis: At the centre of a circular loop, every current element contributes dB along the axis — perfect constructive addition gives B = μ₀I/(2R)

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts current I and loop radius R. The field B at centre updates in real time. Student can also switch to straight-wire mode to see B = μ₀I/(2πr). A field point can be dragged to see how dB direction changes with position.
simulation_states.emphasis: Vary I and R to see how B changes. Compare loop field (B ∝ I/R) with wire field (B ∝ I/r). Drag the field point to see cross-product direction change.

common_misconceptions.belief: The magnetic field due to a current element points radially outward like an electric field from a charge
common_misconceptions.correction: The magnetic field dB is perpendicular to BOTH the current element dl and the position vector r. It is given by the cross product dl × r̂, so it never points radially from the element.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show dl, r, and dB as three mutually oriented vectors — dB is always perpendicular to the plane containing dl and r

common_misconceptions.belief: The field at the centre of a loop is in the plane of the loop, along the wire
trigger_phrases: field loop ke plane mein hota hai
centre pe field wire ki direction mein
common_misconceptions.correction: At the centre of a circular loop, the field is along the AXIS of the loop (perpendicular to its plane). Each current element contributes a dB component along the axis, and all these components add up.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show all dB vectors at the centre pointing along the axis — the in-plane components cancel by symmetry, only the axial component survives

common_misconceptions.belief: Biot-Savart law gives an inverse-cube dependence on distance
trigger_phrases: field r cube pe depend karta hai
common_misconceptions.correction: The Biot-Savart law has 1/r² dependence for the field due to a current element. The 1/r³ appearance sometimes seen is because r̂/r² = r/r³, but the fundamental dependence is inverse-square, analogous to Coulomb's law.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Drag the field point closer and farther — show the field scaling as 1/r², not 1/r³, with numerical readout

legend.meaning: Magnetic field due to current element (T)
Unit vector from dl to field point
